videos.text: no actually quite a lot of things are designed to not be memorizable by humans for example like a JPEG image for example consists of a string of pixels and each of these pixels has a number attached to it right like an RGB number like 0 to 255 and so JPEGs are designed to be memorized by computers but they really can't be memorized by humans because we don't really have the capacity to do that so when I'm talking about memorizability I mean things are sometimes designed to fit inside of the human brain and of course humans can do amazing things they can memorize all kinds of stuff there's a helicopter up there but um but designing something to be memorized is very different and as a matter of fact most of the most of the oldest stories that we have as humans in the world were designed actually to be memorized by people and the structure of those narratives is also a structure that depends on this memorization

videos.desc: what talking to me about computers is like 💙✒️🎶
videos.text: I have this piece that like it's like a square and you click and drag and then you can move these things around and create these little drawings, right? And then on the bottom in every pixel I'm actually gonna encode the steps to reproduce the drawing until the progress bar fills up and then I'm gonna store these steps in the image but also these numbers are gonna be stored on chain so I'm gonna have redundancy because visually these are decodable into the steps so there's gonna be a player program that can load the images and play them back and also the data for that is gonna be stored on chain too so even if you don't have the image but you just have the chain data you can use the player to reconstruct the image

segments.text: I have this piece that like it's like a square and you click and drag and then
you can move these things around and create these little drawings, right? And
then on the bottom in every pixel I'm actually gonna encode the steps to
reproduce the drawing until the progress bar fills up and then I'm gonna store
these steps in the image but also these numbers are gonna be stored on chain so
I'm gonna have redundancy because visually these are decodable into the
steps so there's gonna be a player program that can load the images and
play them back and also the data for that is gonna be stored on chain too so
even if you don't have the image but you just have the chain data you can use
the player to reconstruct the image

videos.desc: why use whistlegraphs?
videos.text: Pretty much everything that we do these days is mediated by data formats. For example, the music you listen to on Spotify is mediated through the mp3 data format. Now that's a format that's designed to make it easy to send audio from this machine, let's say to these headphones or over a network or something like that. But if you wanted to send an audio visual message from human to human and have those humans remember that audio visual message just from brain to brain, then whistle graphing is an excellent choice. Would you like to come down the stairs? I don't want to because I'm too scared. Because in whistle graphing we use hand-eye coordination and singing and drawing to help people actually remember this stuff. And so it's a lot more like poetry than it is like a traditional computer data format, but it does have strict data format rules.

So just because on a whistle graph you can do something like this, when you're told amazing, incredible, and the words that I'm saying don't necessarily have anything to do with high pitch being up here and low pitch being down there or, you know, I'm not on a Cartesian XY grid. It's not a geometric logic, but it is a gestural logic, right? So listen to this. When you're told amazing, incredible, see how the gestures of the lines map to the syllables of the words? videos.desc: #stitch with @julesterpak my elaboration, hope it comes through! 🎈 #media #edutok
videos.text: Hey question, are we amusing ourselves to death? Yes and a bit of no, because Neil Postman who wrote that question, are we amusing ourselves to death back in the 80s, was really talking about television, which is a one to many broadcast medium in which you send a signal out to lots of different people. But the internet, as you know, is a many to many medium where anyone can send anything to anyone. However, because the internet has become mostly centralized, you have social media platforms that kind of send out things to many people, but many people also upload things into those platforms. So if in the 80s we had an entertainment nightmare, today we actually have an edutainment nightmare where you have experts or quote unquote "creators" feeding good information into the machine, and then consumers feeding information into the machine that tells you whether that information is quality or not. And in the end it all kind of supports AI, which leads into the Orwell-Huxley kind of stuff.

videos.desc: Replying to @mynameiskevln “consider this” 🔦 #art #arthistory #whistlegraph
videos.text: Being an artist means you have a kind of flashlight that you can aim at things that people might not usually want to think about or look at. They might even think it's a waste of time to think about or look at the things you're showing them. An artist basically says, "Consider this or look at this." In our case, we took those drawing songs, you know, the like, little circle, little circle, one big circle, like the one that makes a pig. And we decided, what if we took that really seriously? What if we asked people to look closer into why that could be kind of meaningful? And that's what a whistle graph is. It's chilly, but I keep burning For people who aren't around anymore All alone Somebody stomp me out of my misery

segments.text: Being an artist means you have a kind of flashlight
that you can aim at things that people might not usually want to think about or look at.
They might even think it's a waste of time to think about or look at the things you're showing them.

In our case, we took those drawing songs, you know, the like,
little circle, little circle, one big circle, like the one that makes a pig.
And we decided, what if we took that really seriously?

It's chilly, but I keep burning
For people who aren't around anymore
All alone
Somebody stomp me out of my misery

videos.text: And that was when I looked in at my phone. I mean, there really isn't a point, but there is something that makes certain whistle graphs good or bad, and it's the same thing that makes certain kinds of music good or bad, and it's really just how much it matters to people. And, you know, that changes and what matters changes, but folk music is really just a record of all the things that a group of people in history cared about enough to share and keep spreading. So if a whistle graph gets stuck in your mind, then I guess that's part of the point.

videos.desc: artists invent forms, creators just fill them out ⏳ #chatgpt #ai #art
videos.text: So if you're not a stranger to the internet, you've probably seen ChatGPT, and you probably also saw all those cool selfies that people are making, those cool, nice AI selfies. And people are mad about these, right? And you've probably even seen videos about that, about how illustrators are, their works being stolen, or about how programmers, they're gonna kind of be out of a job, or something like that. And I'm here to tell you, as the Jeffrey, the whistle-grapher here, I'm gonna tell you that that is absolutely true. Basically low-level illustration work, low-level programming work, people who make basic APIs, people who make basic logos. This stuff is gonna actually be automated, and it's a little bit of a problem. But if you wanna get ahead of the curve, then you need to start thinking on top of things. You need to start creating something called an art form, as opposed to what you might be doing now, which is making art content. You see, all these algorithms train their data off of specific formats, right? Text, flat JPEG images, these kinds of things, MP3 files, right? These things, artists don't really touch. Artists often don't touch the formats, but they do make content that go in the formats. So because all these systems are designed to train off of existing formats, to stay ahead of the game, you need to make new forms.

videos.text: So this is what my head looks like when I'm reading these Peanuts comics, which I'm sure you know all about, and they were made starting in the 1950s. Of course, using the simplest of technologies, like a fountain pen. And these days, people kind of think, in order to build a world, you kind of need like a game engine or you need to get good at computers or something. But actually what you really need to do is just resign yourself to some basic thing and then just sit around with your brain and maybe have some regular place to put the stuff that you make too. Here's Charlie trying to play the violin. ♪ In the bush outside my house, empty soda cup ♪ ♪ I hope they pick it up ♪ You know, Whistlegraph's only been around for three years, but we still got decades left to go.

videos.desc: Charlie’s Intro, NEW ALB OUT RN 🔥✍️ #drawing #alt #depressed #dance #whistlegraph #music2whistlegraph2
videos.text: My name is Charlie. This is my music. It's good. Some of it is good. Sometimes I think all my music's bad. I pretty much have always thought that my entire life. But luckily I have friends, and friends tell you that your music's not bad. And then friends are also like, "Can you make some music for me for my project where we sing and we draw to our songs?" And they're all scored and it's a score. And but maybe we could use some backing music and then also maybe inspire some freestyling. Little graphing and score stuff and I'm like, "Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah." And then you're happy for a couple moments. And then sometimes the music, like they don't do anything with music, so then maybe they're like, "Oh, but the music could be okay." And you're like, "Oh, I don't really remember even what it sounds like." And then you listen to some because you have listed it for eight months and then it sounds like a lot better. And then you're like, "Okay, we can put some of this out and then people can listen to it and then do whatever they want with it." But it was for like drawing and singing and not really scoring maybe, but just like having some stuff to do. And like sometimes it's fun to do that with music. And I'm really excited that you can listen, but also like if you hate it, that's cool too. And there's a lot of like vibes, you know. So like it's different stuff. If you hate one song, you might like the next song. So just thank you, Alex, to me on deaf. Thank you, WhistleGraph. I hope you don't hate this. Here's 20 songs. Let's go!

Both these have different app client code bases that are probably, we're shared at some point. Now, how did TikTok start? TikTok started because an ex-ByteDance employee went ahead, came to America, started an app called Musically and then later sold that app called TikTok back to the parent Chinese multinational corporation. Now, here's where it gets tricky because the source code of the back end is fucking shared, you guys. It's shared by both TikTok and by Dooyin and ByteDance has got the repos. So basically, here's what happens. All of a sudden, last year, two engineers, two people from ByteDance in China, they doxxed Americans. So they actually looked at American data in China and they doxxed American journalists and this became a news story. videos.desc: Replying to @.void.rat pages 0-13 how’s that? 📖 #sketchbooktour
videos.text: This is my current whistlegraph sketchbook, and here's the inner cover. We recently had these stamps made, so here's a whistlegraph stamp, and here's a drawing that I made from a comment that I received that says, "You sound like you've been crying for years." We're gonna skip page three and go directly to page four, which is butterfly death. And then page five is also about butterfly death. Today I woke and flew out of box and died. And here's a diagram of the butterfly box in a beautiful window. The butterflies fly out of the box and die. Page six is empty, and page seven is these cool ribbon things. At page eight, I was trying to memorize the alphabet backwards. 6 W, S, R, Q, P on Monkey Loopy Cuckoo Junior I have. Yeah, I was trying to create a mnemonic for backwards memorization of the alphabet. Also, there's this alien guy in the corner, and this cool way of doing the side of a girl's hair. Page nine, you'll see there's other examples of the girl side hair thing, which is pretty nifty. On page 11, we have various eye rolls, different types of eye rolling that you can do. Page 12, I was trying to write the ukulele chords for motivation by Sum41, but I lost motivation, so that page is blank. And here's the notes that we did for our visit to the New York Times.

videos.text: Oh, that's very nice, but I don't think you should wear things just because I touched them. For example, I don't think you should wear my sock with these weird faces on it just because I touched my sock, and I don't think you should eat this bread just because I touched this bread, because there's that weird hair there and someone might not like that hair. Generally, objects that people touch, they do have what's often referred to as an aura. So I'm not denying the fact that, you know, everything that one touches has an aura, and then if you touch that thing, then you'll take some of that aura for yourself. But I really don't think it's a great idea to be doing that all the time.

videos.desc: Replying to @mattmgk here’s one way to consider it practically and conceptually #artist #creator #drawing
videos.text: Hi this is Jeffrey the creator of whistlegraph and I can explain to you what it is. Now a while back I wanted to figure out a way to unload graphics. Unload some some graphics out of my brain and onto a page and whenever I unloaded those graphics like a graphics file you know like if you have a PNG file you unload that off of the human brain onto a page and what I was trying to figure out is how can I do that nicely how can I memorize graphics. So what I came up with was a musical scheme and you know we basically every line has a sound associated with it and in the end you have this poetic image. Butterfly fly bing for you guys it's just a costume I put on in my room and those Those steps all come together into an art form.

videos.desc: rehearsing and making boxes #graphics #drawing
videos.text: Hey guys, this is Jeffrey from Whistlegraph and I'm here with Camille and Alex and we're in Green Point practicing one of our favorite pieces called Making Boxes. We've been rehearsing this piece for you guys for about five hours now and we're definitely excited to show it to you. These are some of our previous rehearsals. Are you ready for one? Yeah. Let's do one right here. Making Boxes, Making Boxes, Little Boxes, Bigger Boxes, Bigger Boxes, Bigger Boxes, Bigger Boxes, Bigger Boxes now. Yeah that was a pretty well practiced one. I think we can do better. Let's do another. We're going to do one more for you. Ready? Making Boxes, Making Boxes, Little Boxes, Bigger Boxes, Bigger Boxes, Bigger Boxes, Bigger Boxes, Barely Boxes now. Wow!

excited to show it to you.

Ready?
Making Boxes, Making Boxes, Little Boxes, Bigger Boxes, Bigger Boxes, Bigger Boxes,
Bigger Boxes, Barely Boxes now.
Wow!

videos.text: It's a beautiful day to perform three whistle graphs for you guys. Number one, I'm going to do a sad campfire. It's chilly, but I keep burning For people who aren't around anymore All alone Somebody stomp me out of my misery Number two, I don't need an iPhone. Fun fact, Camille, Jeffrey, and I actually wrote this one together. It was the first three person whistle graph, and it goes like this. I don't need an iPhone I can take your picture In between my fingers I don't need an iPhone And the third whistle graph today is a very scary looking sad mushroom But I'm going to make it cute. I'm going to give it a smile. It's going to be the first smiling mushroom on the account. It goes like this. Keep me in this dark place Because I like it here. Hope you enjoyed three whistle graphs.

videos.desc: a real adventure
videos.text: Hit a hump and plateaued, then I gave up. Now they call me Explody Head. Got my arm out waving, anticipating nothing special. Blocking the doorway, little doggie. Thing like a doggie, well you shut the fuck up. This house that I built, Scott cracks at the side. Brittle like life, and smoothing out. Airpockets, you're no match for my graphic team. My face is full of internet, safely in a bubble that surrounds me. I don't think I want you back in here. Oh no, this emoji defends me in my bubble. Signifying my identity, my identity. It flickers like a shooting star, oh yeah. Do you see?

videos.desc: Replying to @RailofRoads yes i miss them
videos.text: I'm pretty sad about Camille and Alex not being around anymore, and it's been a little over two months now since we sort of started to have a big break from each other. Collaboration is hard, and our collaboration started to sort of show its cracks and become a lot more difficult over a year ago actually, but despite that we kept making work and kept trying to make good work and did make good work, but you know everything kind of needs to come to some kind of end at some point. In terms of sad whistle graphs, also a lot of my whistle graphs have always been sad, and there's a sadness inside of humanity that's always been there, and there's a sadness inside of every person, and unless you give that sadness a shape and give a voice to that sadness somehow, then you're going to be alone in that, and that's really what whistle graphing is for. That's what all art is for. It's for externalizing those feelings, and that's something to celebrate.

videos.desc: feelings about primitive geometric boundaries #circle #triangle #square
videos.text: Did you ever think about how being inside of a circle, being trapped inside of a circle, would be really scary? Because no matter where you go, you're kind of like in the same exact spot. There's no way to differentiate. But then being trapped inside of a square would be a little bit better because there's kind of four places to lose yourself in. But then the worst of all is being trapped inside of a triangle. Because no matter where you look, there's two fucking things right behind you.

videos.desc: welcome to @aesthetic.computer
videos.text: Hi, this is Geoffrey from Whistlegraph, and I'd like to introduce you to Aesthetic Computer. It's a social software machine for media manipulation, and here's what it can do right now. Time to talk backwards. Hello? Wella? Wella? Wella? Wella? Or give it a wave. It even has a camera. You can also text your girlfriend. *sighs* She's always right. I wonder what he thinks though. Damn. Maybe it's time to break up. I want to move some pixels around on my wallpaper, and crop it in order to make a painting. Center it on the screen, and I guess I forgot to log in. Now, yep, there I am. Next, I'm going to use Maya's sparkle brush. I'll get it just right. And add some words. That looks pretty good. And I want it to paint a rainbow on the left side. Cute. I think I'm finished now. Here's my picture, and notice how Aesthetic Computer keeps track of all the steps I typed in. I can tap and hold to save it, send the URL to a friend, or even print it as a sticker. Aesthetic Computer stickers are five and a half inches, and they come in brown envelopes and look like this. You know, I'm going to change my mood. My mood is always visible, right on my profile, along with the last painting that I made. And my profile has a URL too. Before I go, I'm going to make a little tape. And render it as a video. Now I want to post that to TikTok. I'm sure my audience is going to love that. And to close my session, I'm going to log out with bye.

It's sort of, like, more of a shopping platform since they tried to monetize, but it's just amazing that software basically did all of that. You know, like, some people put out some software and then the world started using it and then people started to, like, self-organize into weird commercial structures, but also social structures that I had never seen before in my life. And, um, I think that's why I ended up in the end wanting to make TikTok videos and then wanting to do it with other people because I wanted to take part in what I saw was some kind of strange social and economic reorganization of people who were excited about, uh, software in which their videos were actually being seen by each other. videos.desc: i guess forgot how to breathe like a baby
videos.text: Hi, I'm posting this video as a kind of a time capsule video to follow my weird body noise issues that I started having a year and a half ago in my hand. And I realized very recently that I only ever breathe through my chest like... And I was reading online that people aren't really supposed to breathe that way and when you watch a baby breathe they just breathe through their diaphragm like this deeper way. And so I started practicing that and I don't know how long I've been breathing just out of my chest probably like 10 or 15 years maybe more but here's what happens when I just breathe with my diaphragm. I'll show you. You can hear all the noises and stuff in my back and before I was like forcing those noises but honestly I've been practicing breathing like this for five hours today and it's awesome and I feel I mean it's kind of painful but I also feel a lot better weirdly and I've been reading a lot of information online about how your body stores trauma and stuff like that and yeah I probably just have a lot of that shit inside me so anyway I'll make another video in like a few months and see if these noises go away but I'm gonna keep practicing practicing this every day.
